metadata.description: Anatomy MCQ dataset from Indian medical PG entrance exams (AIIMS, NEET, INI-CET)

questions.question_text: Nucleus pulposus is derived from ____________.
questions.image_description: Diagram of embryonic disc showing labeled structures: 1 (notochord), 2 (ectoderm), 3 (endoderm), 4 (intraembryonic mesoderm)
questions.explanation: Nucleus pulposus is derived from the notochord (marked as 1). The other labelled structures are: 2 - Ectoderm, 3 - Endoderm, 4 - Intraembryonic mesoderm.

questions.question_text: What is the origin of the fibers marked by the arrow?
questions.image_description: Sagittal section of the brain with a yellow arrow pointing to a white fiber tract (fornix)
questions.explanation: The arrow indicates the fornix. It arises from the hippocampus and ends in the mamillary body of the hypothalamus. Fornix is an example of an association fiber. It is supplied by the anterior cerebral artery.

questions.question_text: Among the following, what is the function of the marked muscles?
questions.image_description: Diagram of the mandible showing pterygoid muscles with arrows pointing to the lateral pterygoid muscle
questions.explanation: The muscle marked in the above image is the lateral pterygoid muscle and its primary function is depression of the mandible (opening of the mouth). Bilateral contraction of the lateral pterygoid alone causes depression of the mandible (making it the primary function) whereas protrusion is the result of bilateral contraction of both the medial and lateral pterygoid muscles. The lateral pterygoid has the following actions: Opening of the jaw (depression), protrusion (pulling the mandibular condyle forwards and slightly downwards), and side-to-side movement (grinding food between teeth).

questions.question_text: Which of the marked foramen does the mandibular division of the trigeminal nerve pass through?
questions.image_description: Superior view of the internal surface of the skull base with four foramina labeled A, B, C, D
questions.explanation: The mandibular nerve passes through the foramen ovale which is marked as A in the above image. Other foramina marked: B - Foramen spinosum, C - Internal acoustic meatus, D - Jugular foramen.

questions.question_text: The structure marked with the asterisk in the electron micrograph indicates ____________.
questions.image_description: Electron micrograph of a synapse showing structures marked with asterisk (*) and arrowhead
questions.explanation: The structure marked with the asterisk in the electron micrograph indicates presynaptic secretory vesicles. The structure marked with the arrowhead is postsynaptic density. The secretory vesicles are synthesized by the neurons and are involved in electrochemical signalling in the brain and neuromuscular junctions. On electron microscopy: Mitochondria appear as round or elliptical bodies of 0.5-2.0 micron long. Golgi bodies appear as stacks of flattened membranous cisternae surrounded by clusters of vesicles. Ribosomes may be attached to the membrane of the rough endoplasmic reticulum, or exist as monosomes or polyribosomes associated with mRNA.

questions.question_text: In the image given below, identify the muscle responsible for the opening of the mouth.
questions.image_description: Two diagrams showing chewing muscles (superficial and deep views) with structures labeled A, B, C, D
questions.explanation: In the given image the structure marked B represents the lateral pterygoid muscle which is responsible for the opening of the mouth. Origin - Upper head: Infratemporal surface and infratemporal crest of the greater wing of the sphenoid bone. Lower head: Lateral surface of the lateral pterygoid plate. Insertion: Pterygoid fovea, capsule of the temporomandibular joint, and the articular disc. Nerve supply: Lateral pterygoid nerve (branch of trigeminal nerve). Other structures: A - Temporalis, C - Masseter, D - Medial pterygoid.

questions.question_text: Identify the structure passing through the foramen marked.
questions.image_description: Superior view of skull base with an arrow and question mark pointing to a foramen (superior orbital fissure region)
questions.explanation: The marked foramen is the superior orbital fissure and the abducens nerve passes through it. The abducens nerve is the 6th cranial nerve and arises from the brainstem at the pontomedullary junction. It runs through Dorello's canal, enters the cavernous sinus where it lies lateral to the internal carotid artery, and enters the orbit through the superior orbital fissure within the common tendinous ring to supply the lateral rectus. Other options: Spinal accessory nerve passes through foramen magnum. Ophthalmic artery passes through optic canal. Middle meningeal artery passes through foramen spinosum.

questions.question_text: The talocalcaneonavicular joint is a ________ type of joint.
questions.explanation: The talocalcaneonavicular joint is a ball and socket joint. The ball part is formed by the rounded head of the talus. The socket is formed by 3 structures: Posterior surface of the navicular, Anterior articular surface of the calcaneus, and Upper surface of the plantar calcaneonavicular ligament (spring ligament).

questions.question_text: Which of the following is connected to the structure marked as A in the image below?
questions.image_description: Sagittal section of brain with structure A marked by a blue arrow at the posterior end of the corpus callosum (splenium)
questions.explanation: The structure marked A is the splenium of the corpus callosum and it is connected to the striate cortex. The body of the corpus callosum arches posteriorly and ends as the thickened posterior portion called the splenium. The fibers from the splenium form the forceps major and arch backward into the occipital lobe (striate cortex). The hippocampus is not connected directly to the splenium, but situated inferior to the splenium.

questions.question_text: The structure passing through the central tendon of the diaphragm is:
questions.explanation: The inferior vena cava passes through the central tendon of the diaphragm.

questions.question_text: Shape of Trapezius muscle is ________________
questions.explanation: The trapezius is a broad, flat and triangular muscle. Considering left or right side - triangular shape; considering both sides together - diamond shaped, hence named 'trapezius'.

questions.question_text: All of the following are true about grey rami communicantes except:

options.d: Present medial to white rami communicantes

questions.explanation: The grey rami communicantes are not preganglionic fibers. They are postganglionic fibers. The sympathetic trunks consist of a series of ganglia connected to spinal nerves by white and grey rami communicantes. Preganglionic sympathetic neurons are in the lateral horn of spinal grey matter. They exit via ventral nerve roots and enter the sympathetic trunk in white rami communicantes (preganglionic, myelinated, acetylcholine). They synapse with postganglionic neurons whose axons return to the spinal nerve through grey rami communicantes (postganglionic, unmyelinated, noradrenaline).

questions.question_text: Which of the following represents a holocrine gland?
questions.image_description: Four histopathological images labeled A, B, C, D showing different types of glands
questions.explanation: Image B represents the sebaceous gland, which is a holocrine gland. Holocrine secretions are produced in the cytoplasm and released by rupture of the plasma membrane, destroying the cell. Examples: Sebaceous glands of skin and Meibomian glands of eyelid. Image A: Sweat glands (Eccrine/merocrine). Image C: Mammary gland (Apocrine). Image D: Ceruminous gland (Apocrine).

questions.question_text: Which of the following structures marked in the below image gives rise to the heart in an adult?
questions.image_description: Photograph of a human embryo with structures labeled A, B, C, D
questions.explanation: The structure marked 'C' in the above image depicts the heart bulge (developing heart).

questions.question_text: A 58-year-old woman was undergoing cholecystectomy. During the procedure, the resident accidentally advanced the scissors along the superior border of the epiploic foramen causing injury. Which of the following structures is likely to be injured?
questions.explanation: The superior border of the epiploic foramen (foramen of Winslow) is formed by the caudate lobe of the liver. Epiploic foramen is a vertical slit of about 3 cm behind the free right border of the lesser omentum connecting the lesser sac to the greater sac. Clinical note: Pringle maneuver is a technique where the free margin of the lesser omentum is compressed to obtain rapid control of bleeding from the hepatic artery and portal vein in liver trauma and surgery.

questions.question_text: Which of the following structures does the cell pointed in arrow inhibit?
questions.image_description: Histopathological section of the cerebellum with an arrow pointing to large cells (Purkinje cells)
questions.explanation: The image shows a histopathological section of the cerebellum. The cells marked with the arrow are Purkinje cells and they inhibit deep nuclei of the cerebellum.

questions.question_text: The image below shows the insertion of transversus abdominis muscle. Identify the conjoint tendon:
questions.image_description: Diagram of the anterior abdominal wall showing layers with structures labeled A, B, C, D
questions.explanation: The structure marked C is the conjoint tendon, formed from the lower fibres of internal oblique and the lower part of the aponeurosis of transversus abdominis. It is attached to the pubic crest and extends along the pectineal line. Transversus Abdominis - Origin: internal surface of 7th-12th costal cartilages, thoracolumbar fascia, iliac crest, connective tissue deep to lateral third of inguinal ligament. Insertion: Linea Alba with aponeurosis of internal oblique, pubic crest, and pecten pubis via conjoint tendon. A: Lumbar fascia, B: Transversus Abdominis, D: Inguinal Ligament.

questions.question_text: What represents the marked area in the given histology of lymph node?
questions.image_description: Histological section of lymph node with a blue arrow pointing to an area at the periphery of a follicle
questions.explanation: The area marked is the mantle zone of a lymph node. The mantle zone is produced as surrounding cells are marginalised by the rapidly growing germinal centre. It is populated by mainly quiescent B cells with condensed heterochromatic nuclei and little cytoplasm. The paracortex lies between cortical follicles and the medulla, populated mainly by T cells not organised into follicles.

questions.question_text: A young patient presented with hypoparathyroidism, absent thymus and tetany. Which of the following marked areas in the picture is defective in this condition?
questions.image_description: Diagram of pharyngeal arches/pouches labeled I, II, III, IV with numbered derivatives 1, 2, 3, 4
questions.explanation: The clinical scenario is suggestive of DiGeorge syndrome, caused by defective development of the third and fourth pharyngeal pouch. In the image, '3' points to the thymus (derivative of 3rd pharyngeal pouch). DiGeorge syndrome is caused by hemizygous interstitial deletion of chromosome 22, characterized by conotruncal defects, hypoparathyroidism, and dysmorphic syndrome (velocardiofacial syndrome). Presentations include: Cleft palate, Abnormal facies, Thymic aplasia (T-cell deficiency), Cardiac defects, Hypocalcemia secondary to parathyroid aplasia (CATCH-22).

questions.question_text: A patient came with complaints of inability to move his 4th and 5th digit, cannot hold a pen and he was not able to hold a paper between fingers. Which of the following sites given below is the probable cause of injury to the nerve in question?
questions.image_description: Diagram of the humerus bone with sites labeled A, B, C, D at different locations
questions.explanation: The clinical situation suggests ulnar nerve injury. The most likely site is medial epicondyle (marked as C). Tests for ulnar nerve lesions: Card test positive, Claw hand deformity, Froment's sign positive, Wartenberg sign, Palmaris brevis sign. Positive card test is due to weakness of palmar interrosei causing loss of adduction. Option A: Radial groove (radial nerve). Option D: Neck of humerus (axillary nerve). Option B: Lateral epicondyle (may lead to tardy ulnar nerve palsy but incidence is less).

questions.question_text: Which of the following structures of the scapula is palpable in the infraclavicular fossa?
questions.image_description: Posterior view of the scapula with structures labeled A, B, C, D
questions.explanation: The part marked 'A' is the coracoid process. The apex of the coracoid process lies approximately 2.5cm below the clavicle, immediately lateral to the infraclavicular fossa (deltopectoral triangle). Hence, the coracoid process can be palpated below the clavicle.

questions.question_text: Identify the pelvic diaphragm in the picture given below.
questions.image_description: Cross-sectional diagram of pelvic anatomy with structures labeled A, B, C, D
questions.explanation: The structure marked 'A' is the levator ani muscle which is part of the pelvic diaphragm. Other structures: B - External anal sphincter, C - Internal anal sphincter, D - Pudendal canal. Levator ani and ischiococcygeus form the pelvic diaphragm, delineating the lower limit of the true pelvis. Levator ani is formed by: Pubococcygeus, Iliococcygeus, and Puborectalis.

questions.question_text: What is the root value of cremasteric reflex?
questions.explanation: Cremasteric reflex is mediated by L1 and L2. In this reflex, there is ipsilateral elevation of the testicle following stroking of the medial thigh. Afferent: Femoral branch of genitofemoral nerve. Efferent: Genital branch of genitofemoral nerve. Deep reflexes: Biceps C5,C6; Brachioradialis C5,C6; Triceps C6,C7; Finger flexion C8; Knee jerk L3,L4; Ankle S1,S2; Jaw jerk CN V (Pons). Superficial reflexes: Corneal CN V(A)/CN VII(E); Scapular C5,T1; Abdominal T7-T12; Anal S3,S4; Cremasteric L1,L2; Plantar L5,S1.

questions.question_text: The structure marked with the asterisk in the electron micrograph indicates ____________.
questions.image_description: Electron micrograph of a synapse showing structures marked with asterisk (*) and arrowhead
questions.explanation: The structure marked with the asterisk indicates presynaptic secretory vesicles. The arrowhead marks postsynaptic density. Secretory vesicles are synthesized by neurons and involved in electrochemical signalling in the brain and neuromuscular junctions. On electron microscopy: Mitochondria appear as round or elliptical bodies of 0.5-2.0 micron. Golgi bodies appear as stacks of flattened membranous cisternae. Ribosomes may be attached to rough ER membrane or exist as monosomes/polyribosomes.

questions.question_text: Inferior thyroid artery is the branch of which of the following?
questions.explanation: Inferior thyroid artery is a branch of thyrocervical trunk, which in turn is a branch of the first part of the subclavian artery. The inferior thyroid artery loops upwards anterior to the medial border of the scalenus anterior, turns medially just below the sixth cervical transverse process, and then descends on longus colli to the lower border of the thyroid gland. Relations between the terminal branches and recurrent laryngeal nerve are very variable and of considerable surgical importance.

questions.question_text: Neurovascular bundle is absent in which compartment of the leg?
questions.explanation: Posterior superficial compartment of the leg does not contain any neurovascular bundle. The posterior compartment is divided into deep and superficial parts by the transverse intermuscular septum. Contents of the posterior superficial compartment are gastrocnemius, plantaris, and soleus muscle. Neurovascular components: Anterior compartment - Anterior tibial artery and Deep fibular/peroneal nerve. Posterior deep compartment - Tibial nerve and posterior tibial vessels. Lateral compartment - Superficial peroneal/fibular nerve and peroneal/fibular vessel.

questions.question_text: A patient presents to you with inability to form words but can make noises. He also knows what he wants to say. What area of the brain has been damaged in this patient?
questions.explanation: The clinical scenario is suggestive of Broca's aphasia in which the Broca's area will be affected. In Broca's aphasia (Motor aphasia / Non-fluent aphasia), areas 44 and 45 in the prefrontal and premotor facial regions of the cerebral cortex are affected. The affected individuals will have difficulty forming verbal or written words. The comprehension aspect of speech is preserved but the person cannot repeat or name the words and lacks fluency. Patients in whom the area is severely affected may have very few words to express all of their emotions.

questions.question_text: Identify the wrongly marked structure in the diagram
questions.image_description: Midsagittal section of brain with structures labeled: A-Corpus Callosum, B-Fornix, C-Hypothalamus, D-Cerebellum
questions.explanation: The image shows the midsagittal section of the brain. The wrongly marked structure is C-Hypothalamus. The structure marked as C corresponds to the pituitary gland.

questions.question_text: Loss of flexion at the interphalangeal joint of thumb after supracondylar fracture is due to the involvement of?
questions.explanation: Loss of flexion at the interphalangeal (IP) joint of the thumb is due to the involvement of the anterior interosseous nerve (AIN). Flexion of thumb occurs at 2 joints: IP joint - Flexor pollicis longus (FPL) supplied by the anterior interosseous nerve (a branch of the median nerve, C7 and C8). MCP joint - Flexor pollicis brevis (FPB) supplied by the recurrent motor branch of the median nerve. Deep branch of ulnar nerve causes ulnar claw hand.

questions.question_text: Purkinje cells of cerebellum relay into ________
questions.explanation: Purkinje cells of the cerebellum relay into the dentate nucleus which is one of the deep cerebellar nuclei. The input to the Purkinje cells come from the climbing fibres. The output of Purkinje cells is inhibitory to the deep cerebellar nuclei. Options A and B: Caudate and lentiform nuclei are part of the basal ganglia. Option D: Red nucleus receives afferents from the cerebellum and plays a role in modulating signals to the upper motor neurons of the cerebral cortex.

questions.question_text: Which of the following sensations is not perceived by the spinal nucleus of the trigeminal nerve?
questions.explanation: Proprioception is perceived by the mesencephalic nucleus of the trigeminal nerve, not the spinal nucleus. The trigeminal nerve consists of 4 nuclei: 3 sensory (Mesencephalic, Spinal, and Principal sensory nucleus) and 1 motor. Mesencephalic nucleus receives proprioception from muscles of mastication, facial and extraocular muscles. Principal sensory nucleus receives touch and pressure from the face. Spinal nucleus receives pain and temperature and some fibers of touch. Motor nucleus supplies the muscles of mastication, tensor tympani, tensor veli palatini, mylohyoid and anterior belly of digastric.

questions.question_text: The structure marked in the given picture arises from the ________.
questions.image_description: Coronal section of the brain with an arrow pointing to a white fiber tract (fornix) running between the hemispheres
questions.explanation: This is a coronal section of the brain and the marked structure is the fornix. It arises from the hippocampus and ends in the mammillary body of the hypothalamus.

questions.question_text: Which of the following arteries does not supply the corresponding region of the dura mater?

options.a: Middle meningeal artery – Anterior cranial fossa
options.b: Accessory meningeal branch of maxillary artery – Middle cranial fossa
options.c: Meningeal branch of internal carotid artery – Posterior cranial fossa
options.d: Meningeal branch of anterior and posterior ethmoid artery – Anterior cranial fossa

questions.explanation: The meningeal branch of the internal carotid artery (ICA) does not supply the posterior cranial fossa. It supplies the anterior cranial fossa. The dura mater is supplied by meningeal arteries that pass along the inner periosteum (endosteum) of the skull. Anterior cranial fossa: Meningeal branches of anterior/posterior ethmoid arteries, internal carotid artery, and middle meningeal artery. Middle cranial fossa: Middle meningeal artery, accessory meningeal branches of maxillary artery, ascending pharyngeal artery, branches of internal carotid artery, recurrent branch of lacrimal artery. Posterior cranial fossa: Meningeal branches of occipital artery, posterior meningeal branches of vertebral artery, dorsal meningeal and tentorial arteries from the meningohypophyseal trunk.

questions.question_text: All of the following take part in the formation of the wrist joint, except:
questions.explanation: The distal end of the ulna does not take part in the formation of the radiocarpal joint. The radiocarpal joint (Wrist joint) is a synovial, biaxial, and ellipsoid joint formed by the articulation of: the distal end of the radius, articular disc of the triangular fibrocartilage, and scaphoid, lunate and triquetrum. In the neutral position of the wrist, only the scaphoid and lunate are in contact with the radius and articular disc; the triquetrum comes into apposition with the disc only in full adduction of the wrist joint.

questions.question_text: Which of the following nerves is not involved in olfaction?
questions.explanation: The hypoglossal nerve is purely motor cranial nerve and is not involved in olfaction. The other three cranial nerves — trigeminal, glossopharyngeal, and vagus nerves are mixed cranial nerves and contribute to olfaction. Vagal nerve stimulation modifies the electrical activity of the olfactory bulb.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is the most conspicuous sign of breast cancer?
questions.explanation: The most conspicuous sign of breast cancer is Peau d'orange appearance. Peau d'orange (orange peel like texture) is the edematous swollen and pitted breast skin due to obstruction of the subcutaneous lymphatics.

questions.question_text: Identify the cranial nerve that passes through the marked foramen marked in the image below.
questions.image_description: Superior view of the skull base with an arrow and question mark pointing to a foramen (superior orbital fissure)
questions.explanation: The marked structure is the superior orbital fissure and the oculomotor nerve passes through this fissure. Superior orbital fissure is divided into three compartments by the common tendinous ring. The oculomotor nerve passes through its intermediate compartment.

questions.question_text: Which of the following veins drain into brachiocephalic vein?
questions.explanation: The left superior intercostal vein drains into the left brachiocephalic vein. Tributaries of the brachiocephalic vein: Left and right internal thoracic veins, left and right inferior thyroid veins, left superior intercostal vein drains into the left brachiocephalic vein. The right superior intercostal vein drains into the azygos vein. Option A: Hemiazygous vein drains into the azygous vein. Option B: Superior thyroid veins drain directly into internal jugular veins.

questions.question_text: What is the main action of the muscle marked below?
questions.image_description: Superior view of skull base showing muscles of mastication from above, with an arrow and question mark pointing to a muscle (temporalis)
questions.explanation: The muscle marked in the image is the temporalis and its main action is elevation of the mandible. Other actions: It helps in the side-to-side grinding movements of the mandible. The posterior fibres which are horizontal help in the retraction of the mandible.

questions.question_text: Ligamentum flavum has high levels of:
questions.explanation: Ligamentum flavum has high levels of elastin fibres. The ligamenta flava are a series of ligaments that connect the ventral parts of the laminae of adjacent vertebrae. They predominantly contain elastic tissue and form part of the posterior surface of the vertebral canal. The high content of elastin allows it to be stretched by 80% without damage. The ligamentum flava resist separation of the laminae in flexion and assist in extension back to the anatomical position.

questions.question_text: Which muscle is not involved in the inversion of foot?
questions.explanation: Peroneus longus muscle is not involved in the inversion of the foot. It causes eversion and plantar flexion. Inversion of the foot is a movement in which the medial border of the foot is elevated so that the sole faces medially. Tibialis anterior causes dorsiflexion and inversion. Tibialis posterior causes plantar flexion and inversion. Extensor hallucis longus causes extension of the great toe, dorsiflexion, and inversion (minimal action). Eversion involves peroneus longus, peroneus brevis, and peroneus tertius. These movements take place mainly at the talocalcaneal and talocalcaneonavicular joints.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is normally seen in lumbar spine?
questions.explanation: Lordosis is normally seen in lumbar spine. In adults: The cervical curve is a lordosis (convex forwards), the thoracic curve is a kyphosis (convex dorsally), the lumbar curve is also a lordosis, and the pelvic curve is concave anteroinferiorly.

questions.question_text: Type I collagen is not seen in ________.
questions.explanation: Cartilage is made up of type II collagen. Type I collagen is found in bone, ligament, tendon, and aponeurosis (mnemonic: B-ONE). Type II collagen is found in hyaline and elastic cartilage (Car-Two-Lage). Type III collagen is in reticular tissue (Re-Three-Cular). Type IV collagen is in basement membrane (Floor-Four). There are nearly 28 types of collagen.

questions.question_text: The correct pair of lymph node level in the neck and anatomical boundaries amongst the following are:

options.c: Level 5 - Base of skull to cricoid

questions.explanation: The correct pair of lymph nodes and levels is Level 6 - Hyoid to clavicle. Levels of lymph nodes in neck: Level 1a - Submental, Level 1b - Submandibular, Level 2 - Upper jugular, Level 3 - Middle jugular, Level 4 - Lower jugular, Level 5 - Posterior triangle, Level 6 - Anterior group (Hyoid to clavicle), Level 7 - Mediastinal.

questions.question_text: Meissner corpuscles are present in:
questions.explanation: Meissner corpuscles are present in the papillary dermis. These corpuscles are rapidly adapting mechanoreceptors particularly sensitive to the movement of objects over the skin's surface and low-frequency vibrations. Their acute sensitivity plays a crucial role in tasks such as reading Braille text. It is an elongated encapsulated nerve ending of a large myelinated sensory nerve fiber (type Abeta). Each corpuscle consists of a connective tissue capsule and a central core composed of stacked modified Schwann cells. Meissner's corpuscles are found in nonhairy areas of the skin, especially in fingertips, lips, and other regions where spatial touch perception is highly developed.

questions.question_text: The region marked corresponds to the surface marking for which of the following structures:
questions.image_description: Anterior chest wall with a marked region showing surface anatomy landmarks including costal margin and mid-clavicular line
questions.explanation: The region marked corresponds to the surface marking for the right atrium. The right border of the heart is convex to the right and runs from the third to the sixth right costal cartilages, 1-2 cm lateral to the sternal edge. It corresponds to the right atrium. Upper border corresponds to the left atrium, inferior border to the right ventricle, and left border to the left ventricle.

questions.question_text: The action of the muscle attached to the marked area?
questions.image_description: Femur bone with an arrow pointing to the greater trochanter region
questions.explanation: The image shows the greater trochanter and the muscle fibers of the gluteus medius and gluteus minimus attach(insert) here. Their function is to abduct the thigh and their anterior fibers rotate the thigh medially. Both are innervated by the superior gluteal nerve (L4, L5, S1). Greater trochanter is large and quadrangular at the junction of the neck and shaft. Lesser trochanter is a conical projection, posteromedial to shaft.

questions.question_text: Which of the following finger has two dorsal interossei muscles?
questions.explanation: The Middle Finger has two Dorsal interossei. Dorsal Interossei cause abduction of fingers from the Middle/3rd finger. Palmar interossei are unipennate muscles originating from their own metacarpal bone. Their function is adduction. The middle finger does not need adduction and hence does not have palmar interossei. Dorsal interossei are bipennate muscles originating from the adjacent metacarpal bone. They attach to the middle three fingers on both sides. Nerve supply: Deep branch of the ulnar nerve (C8, T1).

questions.question_text: The structure that divides the liver in Couinaud's classification system is:
questions.explanation: The structure that divides the liver in Couinaud's classification system is the portal vein.

questions.question_text: Which of the following muscle is not supplied by the structure marked A?
questions.image_description: Superior view of the orbital cavity with structure A marked pointing to the oculomotor nerve
questions.explanation: The structure marked is the oculomotor nerve and it does not supply the superior oblique muscle. Superior oblique muscle is supplied by the trochlear nerve. All the extraocular muscles are innervated by the oculomotor (III) nerve except: Lateral rectus which is supplied by abducens (VI) nerve, and Superior oblique which is supplied by trochlear (IV) nerve.

questions.question_text: The wrong statement regarding the levator ani muscle is:
questions.explanation: Levator Ani Muscle is not attached to the pelvic brim. It takes its origin from the body of the pubis, the arcus tendinous of the levator ani (a thickened part of the obturator fascia), and the ischial spine. Insertion: The coccyx and the anococcygeal raphe or ligament. Nerve supply: Branches of the anterior rami of sacral nerves S3 and S4 and the perineal branch of the pudendal nerve. It supports and raises the pelvic floor. Consists of the puborectalis, pubococcygeus, and iliococcygeus.

questions.question_text: In the given section of the heart, match the marked (A, B, C & D) structures.
questions.image_description: Cut and reflected section of the heart showing labeled structures A, B, C, D with matching options: 1-Musculi pectinate, 2-Anterior papillary muscle of left ventricle, 3-Anterior papillary muscle of right ventricle, 4-Anterior leaflet of mitral valve, 5-Membranous part of ventricular septum
questions.explanation: The image shows a cut and reflected section of the heart. The ventricles can be identified based upon the wall thickness. Left ventricle has thicker walls. The correct match is: A-Membranous part of interventricular septum (5), B-Right anterior papillary muscle (cut) (3), C-Left anterior papillary muscle (2), D-Muscle pectinate (1).

questions.question_text: A lesion of the structure marked in the image below causes?
questions.image_description: Ventral view of the brainstem with an arrow and question mark pointing to the pyramid region of the medulla
questions.explanation: The structure marked in the image is the pyramid containing the pyramidal tracts. A lesion here leads to contralateral hemiplegia.

questions.question_text: Identify the structure marked in the image given below:
questions.image_description: Dissection of the palm showing blood vessels, with an arrow pointing to a vessel contributing to the superficial palmar arch
questions.explanation: The structure marked in the image is the ulnar artery, seen contributing to the superficial palmar arch. It passes deep to the Guyon's canal in the wrist and contributes to the superficial and deep palmar arch.

questions.question_text: True regarding the lesion of the structure marked by the arrow is:
questions.image_description: Dissection of the forearm/cubital fossa region with an arrow pointing to a nerve structure
questions.explanation: The structure marked in the image is the radial nerve demonstrated in the cubital fossa. A lesion of the radial nerve leads to a wrist drop. Ape thumb deformity is seen in a lesion of the median nerve. Claw hand deformity is seen in a lesion of the ulnar nerve.

questions.question_text: Which of the following structures given below is related to the structure highlighted (red) in the image?
questions.image_description: 3D model of the pelvis with red highlighted areas on both sides at the ischial spines
questions.explanation: The structure marked in red is the ischial spine and the pudendal nerve is related to it. The pudendal nerve originates from the sacral plexus S2-S4. It exits out of the pelvis by passing through the greater sciatic foramen. It then enters the perineum below the pelvic floor by passing around the ischial spine. The ischial spine is used as a landmark for pudendal nerve block.

questions.question_text: All of the following form the marked structure except:
questions.image_description: Dissection of the dorsum of the hand with an arrow pointing to the dorsal digital expansion of the middle finger
questions.explanation: The structure marked is the dorsal digital expansion of the middle finger. Palmar interossei do not attach to the middle finger and therefore do not contribute to the dorsal digital expansion of the middle finger. Palmar interossei are unipennate muscles originating from their own metacarpal bone. Their function is adduction. The middle finger does not need adduction and hence does not have palmar interossei.

questions.question_text: Which of the following structures of brachial plexus do not give branches?
questions.explanation: The divisions in the brachial plexus do not give any branches. However, the roots, trunks, and cords of the brachial plexus give out direct branches.

questions.question_text: Lesion of the structure marked 'A' causes:
questions.image_description: Dissection of the cubital fossa with structure A marked by a yellow arrow pointing to the median nerve

options.a: Loss of sensation of the nail of index finger
options.c: Loss of sensation at the tip of radial tubercle
options.d: Loss of sensation of the 4th interdigital cleft

questions.explanation: The structure marked 'A' in the diagram is median nerve in the cubital fossa. A lesion of the median nerve causes loss of sensation of the nail of the index finger. Median nerve cutaneous supply: Lateral 2/3rd of the palm by superficial cutaneous branch, and lateral 3½ fingers (palmar aspect and dorsal aspect of nailbeds) by palmar digital nerves. Contents of cubital fossa (from medial to lateral) - MBBR: Median nerve, Brachial artery, Biceps brachii tendon, Radial nerve.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is related to the marked area in the image?
questions.image_description: Left kidney specimen with a red arrow pointing to the upper pole/suprarenal area
questions.explanation: The marked area in the image of the left kidney is related to the stomach. Posterior relations of the kidneys include: One vessel (subcostal vessels), Two ligaments (medial and lateral arcuate ligaments), Three nerves (subcostal, iliohypogastric, and ilioinguinal nerves), Four muscles (diaphragm, psoas major, quadratus lumborum, and transversus abdominis). The right kidney is related to the 12th rib and the left kidney is related to the 11th and 12th ribs.

questions.question_text: The blood supply to the structure marked in the image is by:
questions.image_description: Sagittal section of the brain with a yellow arrow pointing to the fornix
questions.explanation: The marked structure is fornix which is supplied by the anterior cerebral artery. The anteromedial central branches from the anterior cerebral artery supply the fornix along with optic chiasma, lamina terminalis, hypothalamus, para-olfactory areas, and cingulate gyrus.

questions.question_text: Marked structure is derived from the______
questions.image_description: Diagram of the developing heart showing labeled structure A pointing to a region of the interventricular septum
questions.explanation: The structure marked is the membranous part of the interventricular septum. It is derived from endocardial cushions. Endocardial cushions are soft tissue masses that protrude in the atrioventricular and conotruncal regions. Atrioventricular cushions are derived from overlying endocardial cells. Conotruncal cushions are derived from neural crest cells. Endocardial cushions contribute to: Atrial septa, Ventricular septa (membranous portion), Atrioventricular canals, Atrioventricular valves, Aortic and pulmonary channels. Defects in endocardial cushion formation can result in ASDs, VSDs, transposition of great vessels, common truncus arteriosus, and tetralogy of Fallot.

questions.question_text: All of the following supply the marked structure except:
questions.image_description: Photograph of the heart with an arrow pointing to the right ventricle
questions.explanation: The structure marked is the right ventricle. It is not supplied by the diagonal artery. The diagonal artery is a branch of the anterior interventricular artery supplying the left ventricle. This is present in about 30-50% of the population. The right ventricle is mostly supplied by the right coronary artery and its branches such as the marginal artery. The anterior interventricular artery gives off 1-2 anterior right ventricular branches.

questions.question_text: What is the nerve supply of the structure marked in the image below?
questions.image_description: Coronal section of the face showing paranasal sinuses and orbital contents with a question mark pointing to the superior oblique muscle
questions.explanation: The structure marked in the image is the superior oblique muscle. It is innervated by the trochlear nerve. There are seven extraocular muscles present in the eye: Levator palpebrae superioris (elevates the upper eyelids), Four recti muscles (Superior, inferior, medial, and lateral recti), Two obliques (Superior and inferior oblique). The muscles originate from the back of the orbit in a common tendinous ring called the annulus of Zinn and are inserted into the sclera. All the extraocular muscles are innervated by the oculomotor (III) nerve except: Lateral rectus (abducens VI nerve) and Superior oblique (trochlear IV nerve).

questions.question_text: Given is a model of embryological development of the heart. Which structure develops from the area marked as 'A'?
questions.image_description: Model of embryological heart development showing dextro-looping stage with area A marked in the red region (bulbus cordis)
questions.explanation: The structure marked is conus cordis (middle part of bulbus cordis). The infundibulum of the heart develops from this structure. The model shows the embryologic stage where both heart tubes have fused and are undergoing dextro-looping (around day 23). Bulbus cordis gives rise to: proximal 1/3 (conus cordis) → infundibulum/outflow tract of right ventricle; distal 1/3 (truncus arteriosus) → root and proximal portions of aorta and pulmonary artery. Primitive ventricle → trabeculated part of left ventricle. Primitive atrium → common atrium that splits to right and left atrium.

questions.question_text: A muscle is attached to the area marked in the given image. What is the action of this muscle on the hip joint?
questions.image_description: Posterior view of femur with a blue highlighted area at the gluteal tuberosity
questions.explanation: The area marked is the gluteal tuberosity. The muscle inserted here is the gluteus maximus which causes extension and lateral rotation of the hip joint. Gluteus maximus inserts onto iliotibial tract (3/4th part) and deep fibers (1/4th) attach on gluteal tuberosity. It is supplied by the inferior gluteal nerve. It is the chief extensor of the lower limb at the hip joint and additionally causes lateral rotation and abduction.

questions.question_text: Neurotransmitter secreted by the area marked in the given image is?
questions.image_description: Coronal section of the brain with area A marked at the substantia nigra region
questions.explanation: The area marked is substantia nigra (meaning dark substance). The neurotransmitter secreted by this area is dopamine. Substantia nigra is a part of the nigrostriatal pathway. Degeneration of dopaminergic neurons is implicated in Parkinson's disease. Other dopaminergic pathways include: Mesocortical, Mesolimbic, and Tuberoinfundibular.

questions.question_text: Which of the following arteries passes in close proximity to the area marked by the arrow in the given image?
questions.image_description: Lateral view of skull with arrow pointing to the pterion region (circled in blue)
questions.explanation: The area marked by an arrow is pterion and the artery that passes in close proximity is the middle meningeal artery. The pterion is an H shaped sutural junction between four bones of the skull: Frontal bone, Squamous part of the temporal bone, Greater wing of the sphenoid bone, and Parietal bone. It overlies the anterior branch of the middle meningeal artery and lateral fissure of the cerebral hemisphere. The bone is thin here and injury can lead to extradural hemorrhage.

questions.question_text: Where are the Ito cells in the liver located?
questions.explanation: Ito cells (hepatic stellate cells) are located in the Space of Disse in the liver. They store vitamin A and are involved in liver fibrosis when activated.

questions.question_text: Which of the following features differentiates the exocrine pancreas from the parotid gland?

options.a: Basal lamina lining the serous acini
options.c: Presence of serous acini with acidophilic tip

questions.explanation: The exocrine pancreas lacks striated ducts, which are present in the parotid gland. Both are serous glands with similar acinar structure, but the ductal system differs. The pancreas has centroacinar cells that are unique to it.

questions.question_text: Medial lemniscus is a continuation of which of the following structures?
questions.explanation: Medial lemniscus is a continuation of the fasciculus gracilis (and fasciculus cuneatus). These are the dorsal column tracts that carry fine touch, proprioception, and vibration. The fibers synapse in the gracile and cuneate nuclei in the medulla, then cross as internal arcuate fibers to form the medial lemniscus which ascends to the thalamus.

questions.question_text: The type of fibers present in the fornix are:
questions.explanation: The fornix contains both association and commissural fibers. The main body of the fornix carries association fibers from hippocampus to mammillary body. The hippocampal commissure (commissure of the fornix) connects the two hippocampi across the midline, providing commissural fibers.

questions.question_text: Decussation of the superior cerebellar peduncle occurs at which level?
questions.explanation: Decussation of the superior cerebellar peduncle occurs at the level of the midbrain (inferior colliculus level). The superior cerebellar peduncle carries efferent fibers from the dentate nucleus to the contralateral red nucleus and thalamus.

questions.question_text: The pretracheal layer of deep cervical fascia encloses all of the following except:
questions.explanation: The pretracheal layer of deep cervical fascia does not enclose the sternocleidomastoid. SCM is enclosed by the investing layer of deep cervical fascia. The pretracheal layer encloses the thyroid gland, trachea, oesophagus, and infrahyoid muscles (sternohyoid, sternothyroid, thyrohyoid, omohyoid).

questions.question_text: All of the following are the contents of the carotid sheath except?
questions.explanation: The sympathetic chain is not a content of the carotid sheath. It lies posterior to the carotid sheath. Contents of the carotid sheath: Common carotid artery (and internal carotid artery above), Internal jugular vein, Vagus nerve (10th cranial nerve), and deep cervical lymph nodes.

questions.question_text: A patient presented with paradoxical respiration. Which of the following nerves is most likely to be affected?
questions.image_description: Dissection of the neck showing thyroid gland with nerves labeled A, B, C, D
questions.explanation: Paradoxical respiration is caused by phrenic nerve injury leading to diaphragmatic paralysis. The nerve marked D in the image corresponds to the phrenic nerve. The phrenic nerve (C3, C4, C5) is the sole motor supply to the diaphragm. Injury leads to paradoxical movement where the diaphragm moves upward during inspiration.

questions.question_text: Which of the following muscles is attached to the area marked in the image below?
questions.image_description: Skeleton of the hand with a red highlighted area at the base of the first metacarpal (thumb)
questions.explanation: The area marked is the shaft of the first metacarpal bone. Opponens pollicis is attached to this area. Opponens pollicis originates from the flexor retinaculum and trapezium and inserts on the entire length of the first metacarpal shaft on its radial side.

questions.question_text: Which of the following marked structures does not form part of the laryngeal skeleton?
questions.image_description: Sagittal section of the larynx with structures labeled A, B, C, D
questions.explanation: Structure A (hyoid bone) does not form part of the laryngeal skeleton. The laryngeal skeleton consists of cartilages: thyroid, cricoid, arytenoid, corniculate, cuneiform, and epiglottis. The hyoid bone is closely related but is not part of the laryngeal skeleton itself.

questions.question_text: Identify the foramen marked in the image below.
questions.image_description: Superior view of the sphenoid bone with a red arrow pointing to a foramen
questions.explanation: The foramen marked in the image is the foramen rotundum. It transmits the maxillary nerve (V2 branch of trigeminal nerve). Foramen rotundum is located in the greater wing of the sphenoid bone and opens into the pterygopalatine fossa.

questions.question_text: Reticular fibres are not present in which of the following structures?
questions.explanation: Reticular fibres are not present in the thymus. The thymus has an epithelial framework (epithelioreticular cells) rather than a reticular fiber framework. Reticular fibers form the supporting framework of lymph nodes, spleen, and bone marrow.

questions.question_text: Which of the following muscles is not involved in the overhead abduction of the arm?
questions.explanation: Pectoralis major is not involved in overhead abduction of the arm. It is an adductor, medial rotator, and flexor of the arm. Overhead abduction (above 90 degrees) requires: Deltoid and supraspinatus (0-90 degrees), then Serratus anterior and Trapezius rotate the scapula to allow abduction beyond 90 degrees.

questions.question_text: The structures marked in the image below are found in the epithelial lining of all of the following sites, except:
questions.image_description: Histological section showing ciliated pseudostratified columnar epithelium with goblet cells, arrow pointing to cilia
questions.explanation: The structures marked are cilia. The epididymis has stereocilia (long non-motile microvilli), not true cilia. True cilia are found in the oviduct (fallopian tube), bronchi, and ependyma (lining of brain ventricles). Stereocilia in the epididymis help in absorption of fluid and are structurally different from true motile cilia.

questions.question_text: Which of the following bony structures is present behind the marked area?
questions.image_description: Lateral view of the skull with an arrow pointing to a region near the orbit/pterion area and a circled structure
questions.explanation: The bony structure present behind the marked area is the lesser wing of sphenoid.

questions.question_text: All of the following nerves are involved in the movement of the eyeball except:
questions.image_description: Diagram showing cranial nerves exiting the cranial cavity with structures labeled A, B, C, D
questions.explanation: Structure A represents the optic nerve (CN II) which is not involved in the movement of the eyeball. It carries visual sensory information. The nerves that move the eyeball are: Oculomotor (CN III), Trochlear (CN IV), and Abducens (CN VI).

questions.question_text: The middle radioulnar joint is an example of a:
questions.explanation: The middle radioulnar joint is a fibrous joint (syndesmosis). It is formed by the interosseous membrane between the radius and ulna. The superior radioulnar joint is a pivot synovial joint and the inferior radioulnar joint is also a pivot synovial joint.

questions.question_text: What is the action of the muscle at the hip, which is attached to the marked area in the image below.
questions.image_description: Photograph of the proximal femur with an arrow pointing to the lesser trochanter area
questions.explanation: The marked area is the lesser trochanter. The iliopsoas muscle inserts here. The iliopsoas is a powerful flexor of the hip joint. However, looking at the answer key (b), this corresponds to extension and lateral rotation, which would relate to the gluteus maximus inserting on the gluteal tuberosity nearby.

questions.question_text: Identify the level of the cross-section shown in the image below.
questions.image_description: Axial cross-section of the thorax showing heart chambers, great vessels, and vertebral body
questions.explanation: The cross-section shown is at the level of T3-T4. At this level, the aortic arch, superior vena cava, trachea, and esophagus are visible. The arch of the aorta begins and ends at the level of the sternal angle (T4 vertebral level).

questions.question_text: A patient is unable to abduct his arm. Which of the following root values is likely to be affected?
questions.explanation: C5 root value is likely to be affected in inability to abduct the arm. The deltoid (main abductor) is supplied by the axillary nerve (C5, C6), and the supraspinatus (initiates abduction) is supplied by the suprascapular nerve (C5, C6). C5 is the primary root for both.

questions.question_text: Identify the thickened nerve marked in the arrow in the image shown below:
questions.image_description: Two photographs of the neck showing a thickened nerve visible over the sternocleidomastoid muscle, with arrows pointing to it (L and R views)
questions.explanation: The thickened nerve marked is the great auricular nerve. The thickened nerve visible below it is the transverse cervical nerve. Nerve thickening in this case is likely due to leprosy. The great auricular nerve is the largest ascending branch of the cervical plexus, arising from C2 and C3. It winds over the posterior border of SCM and ascends upwards. Anterior branch supplies facial skin over the parotid gland. Posterior branch supplies skin over the mastoid process, posteroinferior part of the auricle, and concha. Clinical: In leprosy, the nerve is palpable over the upper part of SCM. Frey's syndrome involves aberrant connection between the auriculotemporal nerve and great auricular nerve causing gustatory sweating.

questions.question_text: Broca's area is situated in the __________.
questions.explanation: Broca's area is situated in the inferior frontal gyrus. Broca's motor speech area (Brodmann areas 44, 45) is located in the inferior frontal gyrus. Wernicke's area (area 22) is located in the superior temporal gyrus.

questions.question_text: Which of the following cranial nerve nuclei is not a part of the special visceral afferent (SVA) column?
questions.explanation: The trochlear nerve nucleus is a part of the general somatic efferent (GSE) column, not the special visceral afferent column. The trochlear nerve is a motor nerve that innervates the superior oblique muscle in the orbit. The neurons of the GSE column innervate the extrinsic ocular muscles and muscles of the tongue (CN III, IV, VI, and XII). The SVA column includes facial (VII), glossopharyngeal (IX), and vagus (X) nerves.

questions.question_text: The joint between the attachment of the 8th and 9th rib to the 7th rib is a:
questions.explanation: The interchondral joint between the 8th and 9th rib with the 7th rib is a synovial joint. Joints in the thoracic cavity include: Synarthrosis (1st sternocostal joint), Synchondrosis/primary cartilaginous (costochondral joints), Symphysis/secondary cartilaginous (manubriosternal and xiphisternal joints), and Synovial joints (interchondral, sternocostal, costovertebral, costotransverse). Note: The interchondral joint between the 9th and 10th cartilages is never synovial and sometimes absent.

questions.question_text: Which is the last fontanelle to close?
questions.explanation: The anterior fontanelle is the last fontanelle to close, at around 18-24 months of age. Fontanelles of the skull: Anterior (between frontal and parietal bones, closes 18-24 months), Posterior (sagittal and lambdoid sutures, closes 2-3 months), Sphenoid (at pterion, closes 2-3 months), Mastoid/Posterolateral (intersection of temporal, parietal, and occipital bones, closes 6-18 months, average 12 months).

questions.question_text: Which among the following is incorrect regarding Sibson's fascia?

options.a: Attached to the inner border of 2nd rib

questions.explanation: Sibson's fascia is attached to the inner border of the 1st rib, not the 2nd rib. Sibson's fascia (suprapleural membrane) serves as a plane of separation between the lower neck and the thorax and covers the apical part of the lung. It is attached to the inner border of the 1st rib and posteriorly to the anterior border of the transverse process of the C7 vertebra. It is strengthened by muscular fibers derived from the scalene muscles. The subclavian vessels lie on top of it.

questions.question_text: The structure not passing through the aortic opening of the diaphragm is:
questions.explanation: The vagal trunk does not pass through the aortic opening of the diaphragm. It passes through the esophageal opening at the level of T10 vertebra. The aortic opening (T12 level) transmits: Aorta, Thoracic duct, and Azygos vein.

questions.question_text: The hard palate contains _________

options.a: Keratinised epithelium, submucosa, minor salivary glands
options.b: Keratinised epithelium, absent submucosa, no salivary glands
options.c: Non-keratinised epithelium, submucosa, minor salivary glands
options.d: Non-keratinised epithelium, absent submucosa, minor salivary glands

questions.explanation: The hard palate contains keratinised stratified squamous epithelium, has a submucosa, and contains minor salivary glands. The keratinised stratified squamous epithelium has regional variations and may be orthokeratinised or parakeratinised. The bony structure is covered by a firmly attached mucosa in the central part. In the lateral parts, there is a submucosal layer containing blood vessels. Minor mucous type salivary glands are present in the submucosa in the posterior part of the hard palate.

questions.question_text: What is the function of the muscle marked as A in the image below, at the metacarpophalangeal joint?
questions.image_description: Dissection of the palm showing hand muscles with structures labeled A, B, C, D. A points to the lumbrical muscles
questions.explanation: The muscle marked 'A' is lumbricals and they cause flexion at the metacarpophalangeal joints. Lumbricals are intrinsic muscles of the hand that flex the MCP joints and extend the interphalangeal joints (PIP & DIP). They originate from flexor digitorum profundus. The lateral two are unipennate, supplied by the median nerve. The medial two are bipennate, supplied by the ulnar nerve.

questions.question_text: Which among the following is a tensor of vocal cords?
questions.explanation: Cricothyroid is a tensor of vocal cords. It tilts the thyroid cartilage forward on the cricoid, increasing the distance between the vocal process and the thyroid angle, thus tensing the vocal cords. It is the only intrinsic laryngeal muscle supplied by the external laryngeal nerve (branch of superior laryngeal nerve). All other intrinsic laryngeal muscles are supplied by the recurrent laryngeal nerve.

questions.question_text: The nerve of the second branchial arch is:
questions.explanation: The facial nerve is the nerve of the 2nd pharyngeal arch. Each pharyngeal arch is characterized by its own muscular components. The muscular components of each arch have their own cranial nerve. Wherever the muscle cells migrate, they carry their nerve component with them. The neurons of the 5th, 7th, 9th, and 10th cranial sensory ganglia that supply the pharyngeal arch structures are derived from ectodermal placodes and from the neural crest cells.

questions.question_text: Which of the following statements is true about the iliopubic tract?

options.a: It is a thickening of the lumbar fascia
options.b: It lies anterior to the Poupart's ligament
options.c: It inserts into the pubic tubercle in front of the conjoint tendon
options.d: Its fibers fan out with fascia iliaca laterally

questions.explanation: The fibers of the iliopubic tract fan out with fascia iliaca laterally. The iliopubic tract is a thickening of the transversalis fascia lying immediately posterior to the inguinal (Poupart's) ligament. It extends from the anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS) superolaterally and arches across the front of the femoral sheath. Medially, its fibres insert by a broad attachment into the pubic tubercle and pectineal line, behind the conjoint tendon. Laterally, its fibres fan out within the fascia transversalis and fascia iliaca without bony attachment to the iliac spines. It is an important landmark in laparoscopic hernia surgeries.

questions.question_text: Ureteric bud develops from:
questions.explanation: Ureteric bud develops from the Mesonephric duct. Intermediate mesoderm develops into the Genital ridge and Nephrogenic cord. Nephrogenic cord further develops into Pronephros, Mesonephros and Metanephros. Ureteric bud develops from mesonephros which later fuses with Mesonephric duct. Metanephros proliferates around the ureteric bud forming metanephric blastema (definitive kidneys). Metanephric blastema gives rise to Bowman's capsule, proximal convoluted tubule, loop of Henle, and distal convoluted tubule. Ureteric bud gives rise to collecting tubules, renal papillae, calyces, renal pelvis, and ureter.

questions.question_text: The heart starts to beat in the ______ week of development.
questions.explanation: The heart starts to beat in the 4th week of development.

questions.question_text: The movements at the ____________ joint permit a person to look to the right and left.
questions.explanation: The movements at the atlanto-axial joint allow a person to look left and right. The atlanto-axial joints include 3 synovial joints: Median atlanto-axial joint (central pivot joint between the dens of the axis and a ring formed by the anterior arch and transverse atlantal ligament) and Lateral atlanto-axial joints (plain synovial joints between lateral masses of atlas and upper facets of the body of axis). Movements at the atlanto-axial joint almost exclusively is rotation around the axis, which helps side-to-side rotation of the head. The normal range of atlanto-axial rotation is about 40°.

questions.question_text: All of the following are true about the right coronary artery except ______

options.a: It's diameter is less than left coronary artery
options.b: It arises from the anterior aortic sinus
options.c: It gives rise to circumflex coronary branch
options.d: Right conal artery is it's first branch

questions.explanation: The right coronary artery does not give rise to the circumflex coronary artery. The right coronary artery arises from the anterior aortic sinus (right coronary sinus) of the ascending aorta. It passes anteriorly and descends vertically in the coronary sulcus between the right atrium and the right ventricle. On reaching the inferior margin, it turns posteriorly and continues in the sulcus onto the diaphragmatic surface and the base of the heart. The diameter of the right coronary artery is less than that of the left coronary artery. The right conal artery is usually the first branch of the right coronary artery. The circumflex coronary artery is a branch of the left coronary artery and is the exclusive supplier to the right bundle branch.

questions.question_text: What is the basis for the formation of omphalocele?

options.b: Physiological hernia failing to go back

questions.explanation: Omphalocele is protrusion of abdominal contents through the umbilical ring. It is due to failure of physiological hernia to reduce into the abdominal cavity, which results in non-closure of the umbilical ring. An omphalocele may contain stomach, liver, and intestines, and these contents are enclosed in a sac. Omphalocele is usually associated with other congenital anomalies (e.g., trisomy 13, trisomy 18, or Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome) and with increased levels of alpha-fetoprotein. Note: In gastroschisis, there is an absence of a membranous sac and is paraumbilical.

questions.question_text: Which fibre is marked by the arrow in the image given below?
questions.image_description: Horizontal section of cerebral hemispheres showing the internal capsule, caudate nucleus, lentiform nucleus, and thalamus. An arrow points to the internal capsule region between the lentiform nucleus and the thalamus/caudate nucleus.
questions.explanation: The marked structure is the internal capsule, which is a form of projection fiber. Projection fibers connect the cerebral cortex with subcortical structures and the spinal cord. The internal capsule is a white matter structure situated between the caudate nucleus and thalamus medially, and the lentiform nucleus laterally.

questions.question_text: Which nerve passes through the foramen marked in the image below?
questions.image_description: 3D rendering of a mandible (jawbone) with a red marker highlighting the mandibular foramen on the medial surface of the ramus.
questions.explanation: The marked structure is the mandibular foramen and the inferior alveolar nerve passes through it. The inferior alveolar nerve (IAN), a branch of the mandibular nerve (CN V3), descends behind the lateral pterygoid. At the lower border of the muscle, the nerve passes between the sphenomandibular ligament and the mandibular ramus and enters the mandibular canal via the mandibular foramen. The mandibular foramen is located above the mandibular angle, the opening through which the IAN and the vessels of the mandible pass. The IAN continues its course through the mandibular canal and then branches off to supply the lower teeth, gums, and lower lip.

questions.question_text: Boundaries of Calot triangle are formed by all except:
questions.explanation: The gall bladder does not form a boundary of Calot's triangle. Calot's triangle is bounded by the 3C's: Medially - Common hepatic duct, Inferiorly - Cystic duct, Superiorly - Cystic artery.

questions.question_text: Area marked in the given image is supplied by dermatome number?
questions.image_description: Diagram of the anterior trunk showing dermatome distribution with colored horizontal bands. A question mark arrow points to the area at the level of the umbilicus (highlighted in pink/red).
questions.explanation: The marked dermatome which includes the umbilicus is supplied by T10. The dermatomal distribution of the abdominal region is as follows: T7, T8, T9 - supplies skin from the xiphoid process to just above the umbilicus. T10 - supplies skin around the umbilicus. T11, T12 - supplies skin below the umbilicus up to the suprapubic region. L1 - supplies skin around the genitalia and upper medial thigh.

questions.question_text: The arrow in the image denotes which of the following muscles?
questions.image_description: Anatomical illustration of the posterior forearm and hand showing extensor muscles. An arrow with a question mark points to a muscle on the lateral (radial) side of the forearm near the elbow region.
questions.explanation: The arrow shows extensor carpi radialis longus. It originates from the distal third of the lateral supracondylar ridge of the humerus and inserts at the second metacarpal (radial side). It is innervated by the radial nerve, C6, and C7. The vascular supply is by radial recurrent artery and it is responsible for the extension and abduction of the wrist joint.

questions.question_text: What is the nerve supply of the structure marked in the image below?
questions.image_description: Anatomical illustration of the palmar surface of the hand showing superficial muscles. An arrow with a question mark points to the second lumbrical muscle.
questions.explanation: The marked structure is the second lumbrical which is supplied by the median nerve. The lateral two lumbricals are supplied by the median nerve and the medial two are supplied by the ulnar nerve. Function of these muscles is flexion at the metacarpophalangeal joints and extension at both the interphalangeal joints.

questions.question_text: Nerve supply of the area marked by arrow in the image is:
questions.image_description: Diagram of the hand and fingers showing cutaneous nerve supply zones color-coded in different colors. An arrow points to the dorsal/lateral area of the hand and proximal fingers supplied by the radial nerve.
questions.explanation: The area marked in the image is supplied by the radial nerve. The radial nerve supplies the skin over the dorsum of the hand on the lateral (radial) side, including the dorsal aspect of the thumb, index finger, middle finger, and lateral half of the ring finger up to the proximal phalanges.

questions.question_text: Cholecysto-vena caval line separates which of the following?

options.b: Porta hepatis and inferior vena cava
options.d: Right and left lobe of liver

questions.explanation: Cholecysto-vena caval line (Cantlie line) separates right and left lobe of the liver. Cantlie line is an imaginary line that divides the liver into 2 planes. It extends from the midpoint of the gallbladder fossa at the inferior margin of the liver back to the midpoint of the inferior vena cava (IVC) and contains the middle hepatic vein.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is false about skin?

options.b: Skin accounts for 15% of total body weight
options.d: Dermis is made up of type 1 and type 3 collagen

questions.explanation: Dermis is derived from mesoderm and epidermis is derived from ectoderm. The statement that dermis is derived from ectoderm is false.

questions.question_text: All of the following structures are derived from the aponeurosis of the external oblique muscle except:
questions.explanation: Linea semilunaris is derived from the aponeurosis of the internal oblique muscle. Pectineal ligament, inguinal ligament and lacunar ligament are derived from the external oblique aponeurosis.

questions.question_text: Which of the following lobes of the prostate forms the uvula vesicae?
questions.explanation: The median lobe of the prostate forms the uvula vesicae. Uvula vesicae is an elevation in the mucous membrane of the urinary bladder, behind the internal urethral orifice. It is often enlarged in benign prostatic hypertrophy.

questions.question_text: Foot drop is caused by damage to:
questions.explanation: Foot drop is caused by damage to the common peroneal nerve. The common peroneal nerve is a component of the sciatic nerve. In the posterior thigh, it supplies the short head of the biceps femoris. It curves around the neck of the fibula deep to peroneus longus and divides into superficial and deep peroneal nerves. The superficial peroneal nerve innervates peroneus longus and peroneus brevis (evertors of the foot) and skin over the dorsal area of the foot except for the first webspace and lateral side of the little toe. The deep peroneal nerve innervates all muscles of the anterior compartment (dorsiflexors of the foot), extensor digitorum brevis, first two dorsal interossei muscles, and skin between great and second toes. Hence, injury to the common peroneal nerve leads to: weakness of dorsiflexion (foot drop), loss of eversion, loss of extension of toes, and sensory loss over the anterior and lateral side of the leg and dorsum of the foot.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is true regarding De Quervain's tenosynovitis?

options.a: Fingers are held in mild extension
options.c: Most common involvement is index finger

questions.explanation: De Quervain's disease is a stenosing tenosynovitis of the extensor tendons of the first dorsal compartment, namely extensor pollicis brevis (EPB) and abductor pollicis longus (APL). It is prominent among females, especially pregnant women. Most commonly the thumb is involved. Finkelstein's test: On grasping the patient's thumb and quickly abducting the hand ulnar ward, the pain over the radial styloid tip is excruciating. It is the most pathognomonic objective sign. Treatment: Majority of patients are treated medically. Only few require the release of the 1st dorsal compartment which houses the APL and EPB tendons.

questions.question_text: Identify the structure marked B in the image given below:
questions.image_description: Midsagittal section of the brain showing labeled structures: A (Corpus Callosum), B (structure below corpus callosum), C (Pituitary Gland), and D (Cerebellum). Structure B is located beneath the corpus callosum in the region of the fornix.
questions.explanation: The image shows a midsagittal section of the brain. The structure marked B is the Fornix. A = Corpus Callosum, B = Fornix, C = Pituitary Gland, D = Cerebellum. The fornix is an arched bundle of white matter fibers that connects the hippocampus to the mammillary bodies and is part of the limbic system.

questions.question_text: 1st pharyngeal arch gives rise to all except:
questions.explanation: Levator veli palatini is not derived from the first pharyngeal arch. All soft palate muscles including levator veli palatini are derived from the 4th pharyngeal arch and are supplied by the vagal plexus. The first pharyngeal arch gives rise to: muscles of mastication (temporalis, masseter, lateral and medial pterygoids), mylohyoid, anterior belly of digastric, tensor tympani, tensor veli palatini, and anterior 2/3 of tongue.

questions.question_text: Which of the following forms the lateral boundary of the anatomical snuff box?

options.a: Extensor pollicis brevis and abductor pollicis longus
options.b: Extensor pollicis longus and abductor pollicis brevis
options.c: Extensor pollicis longus and extensor pollicis brevis
options.d: Abductor pollicis longus and abductor pollicis brevis

questions.explanation: The lateral boundary of the anatomical snuff box is formed by extensor pollicis brevis and abductor pollicis longus. Boundaries of the anatomical snuffbox: Lateral wall - Abductor pollicis longus (APL) and Extensor pollicis brevis (EPB). Medial wall - Extensor pollicis longus (EPL). Roof - Cephalic vein and superficial branch of the radial nerve. Floor - Radial styloid, scaphoid, trapezium, and 1st metacarpal. Only content: Radial artery.

questions.question_text: Identify the cell marked in the histological image of the cerebellum shown below:
questions.image_description: Histological image of the cerebellum stained with H&E showing layers of the cerebellar cortex. An arrow points to large flask-shaped cells at the junction between the molecular and granular layers - Purkinje cells.
questions.explanation: The cells marked in the histological image are Purkinje cells. Purkinje cells are flask-shaped cells present in the Purkinje cell layer of the cerebellum. They are evenly spaced and arranged in a single layer. A dendrite arises from the neck of each cell, which passes upward to enter the molecular layer and branches extensively to form a dendritic tree. The axon passes downwards, crosses the granular cell layer to enter the white matter. Axons of Purkinje cells are the only efferents of the cerebellar cortex. Purkinje cells are inhibitory and use GABA as their neurotransmitter. Purkinje cells and neurons in pyramidal cell layers of hippocampus and cerebral cortex are the most sensitive neurons to hypoxia.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is not used for the identification of the facial nerve trunk?
questions.explanation: The inferior belly of the omohyoid muscle is not used as an anatomical landmark for the identification of the facial nerve trunk. Methods for localization of the facial nerve trunk include: Anterograde method using tragal pointer/Conley's pointer, posterior belly of digastric, squamotympanic fissure, styloid process, and mastoid process. Retrograde method uses the main branches (buccal branch near parotid gland, marginal mandibular branch near facial vessels) traced proximally to identify the trunk.

questions.question_text: The false statement about fetal circulation is:

options.a: Anatomical closure of foramen ovale is by 3 to 5 days
options.b: Anatomical closure of ductus venosus is by 3 to 7 days
options.d: Anatomical closure of ductus arteriosus is by 2 to 3 weeks

questions.explanation: The anatomical closure of the foramen ovale takes 1 month to a year, not 3 to 5 days. Fetal circulatory adjustments after birth: Ductus venosus - functional closure after removal of the placenta, anatomical closure by 7 days. Foramen ovale - functional closure at birth after breathing is initiated, anatomical closure takes months to years. Ductus arteriosus - functional closure by 10-15 hours, anatomical closure by 10-21 days.

questions.question_text: A tumor of the uncinate process of the pancreas affects which of the following vessels?
questions.explanation: A tumor of the uncinate process of pancreas affects the superior mesenteric artery. The superior mesenteric artery arises from the abdominal aorta. It lies first behind the body of the pancreas and then in front of the uncinate process. Therefore, a tumor in the uncinate process would affect the superior mesenteric artery.

questions.question_text: A patient presented with clinical features of ataxia and incoordination. This is due to thrombosis of which artery?
questions.explanation: Thrombosis of the superior cerebellar artery causes cerebellar lesion which presents with ataxia and incoordination. Blood supply of the cerebellum includes the superior cerebellar artery, posterior inferior cerebellar artery, and anterior inferior cerebellar artery.

questions.question_text: Which of the following structures prevents the upward extension of thyroid swelling through its attachment to the thyroid cartilage?
questions.explanation: Sternothyroid and sternohyoid prevent the upward extension of the thyroid swelling through their attachment to the thyroid cartilage. Pretracheal fascia encloses the thyroid gland and is attached to the larynx, responsible for the movement of the enlarged thyroid gland on deglutition. The thyrohyoid membrane is a fibro-elastic ligament arising from the superior margin of the thyroid cartilage to the hyoid bone, facilitating upward movement of the larynx during swallowing. The ligament of Berry (suspensory ligament) extends from the cricoid cartilage to the thyroid and prevents the thyroid gland from sinking into the mediastinum.

questions.question_text: A patient presented with the extension of MCP joint and flexion of IP joints. Which muscle is affected?
questions.explanation: The lumbricals and interossei muscles are affected. These muscles, through their attachments to the dorsal digital expansions, cause flexion of the metacarpophalangeal (MCP) joints and extension of the interphalangeal (IP) joints. In cases of ulnar nerve injury, these muscles may get atrophied leading to extension of MCP joints and flexion of IP joints (claw hand). The ulnar nerve innervates all the intrinsic muscles of the hand, except for the first and second lumbrical, abductor pollicis brevis, opponens pollicis, and part of flexor pollicis brevis.

questions.question_text: Meiosis occurs in:
questions.explanation: Meiosis occurs in the adult ovary. Meiosis takes place only in germ cells and results in the formation of haploid gametes. Meiosis requires two cell divisions, meiosis I and meiosis II, to reduce the number of chromosomes to the haploid number of 23. Spermatogenesis begins at puberty; primordial germ cells (PGCs) remain dormant in the testis until puberty. Adrenal and hypothalamus are somatic cells that only undergo mitosis, not meiosis.

questions.question_text: The long left recurrent laryngeal nerve is due to the persistence of which arch artery?
questions.explanation: The long left recurrent laryngeal nerve is due to the persistence of the left 6th arch artery. During development, the laryngeal nerves initially arise below the 6th arch artery and cross here to innervate the intrinsic muscles of the larynx. As the larynx moves cranially relative to the aortic arch arteries, the recurrent laryngeal nerves on the right and left sides are caught under the 6th arch arteries. On the right side, the distal 6th arch artery disappears, so the right recurrent laryngeal nerve is caught under the 4th arch artery (future right subclavian artery). On the left side, the 6th arch artery persists as the ductus arteriosus (later ligamentum arteriosum), so the left recurrent laryngeal nerve is caught under it and has a relatively long course.

questions.question_text: The muscle marked by the arrow in the image below is innervated by the________.
questions.image_description: Posterior view of the upper back and shoulder region showing superficial muscles. An arrow with a question mark points to the levator scapulae muscle on the upper lateral aspect of the back near the neck.

options.c: From the dorsal rami of C1

questions.explanation: The muscle marked in the image is levator scapulae, which is innervated by the dorsal scapular nerve. The appendicular group of back muscles and their innervations include: Trapezius - Accessory nerve, Latissimus dorsi - Thoracodorsal nerve, Levator scapulae - Dorsal scapular nerve, Rhomboid major and minor - Dorsal scapular nerve. The suprascapular nerve innervates the supraspinatus and infraspinatus muscles. The subscapular nerves supply the teres major and subscapularis muscles.

questions.question_text: A patient presented with weakness of the right side of the face with the loss of pain and temperature. Pain and temperature sensation of the left leg is also lost. The lesion is most likely located at?
questions.explanation: The clinical scenario is suggestive of lateral pontine syndrome which occurs due to a lesion at lateral pons. Lateral pontine syndrome occurs due to involvement of the anterior inferior cerebellar artery. Ipsilateral manifestations include: CN VII facial paralysis, CN V loss of pain and temperature sensation over the face, vestibular nerve/nucleus involvement (nystagmus, vertigo), auditory nerve/cochlear nucleus (deafness, tinnitus), middle cerebellar peduncle (ataxia).
