Contralateral manifestations include: spinothalamic tract loss of pain and temperature sensation over the body.

questions.question_text: Ligation of common hepatic artery will impair blood supply in:

options.a: Right gastric and right gastroepiploic artery
options.b: Right gastric and left gastric artery
options.c: Right gastroepiploic and short gastric vessels
options.d: Right gastric and short gastric vessels

questions.explanation: Ligation of the common hepatic artery will cause impairment in the blood supply through its branches, i.e., right gastric artery and right gastroepiploic artery. The arterial supply of the stomach is contributed exclusively by the branches of the celiac trunk: Left gastric artery supplies the lesser curvature. Splenic artery gives short gastric arteries (fundus) and left gastroepiploic artery (greater curvature). Common hepatic artery gives right gastric artery (lesser curvature) and gastroduodenal artery, from which the right gastroepiploic artery arises (greater curvature).

questions.question_text: The type of joint marked in the image below is _________:
questions.image_description: 3D rendering of a thoracic vertebra showing the costovertebral articulation. A bracket with a question mark indicates the costotransverse joint between the rib tubercle and the transverse process.
questions.explanation: The joint marked in the image is the costotransverse joint, which is a synovial type of joint. The various types of joints in the thoracic wall include: Synovial joints - Costovertebral joint, Costotransverse joint, Interchondral joints. Primary cartilaginous joint/Synchondrosis - Costochondral joint. Secondary cartilaginous/Symphyses joint - Manubriosternal and xiphisternal joints. The joint between the 1st costal cartilage and manubrium is a fibrocartilaginous joint (synarthrosis). The joints between the 2nd to 7th costal cartilages and the sternum are synovial.

questions.question_text: Identify the sign shown in the image below:
questions.image_description: Clinical photograph of a patient's arm showing a prominent bulge in the anterior arm during elbow flexion, characteristic of the Popeye sign due to rupture of the long head of biceps brachii tendon.
questions.explanation: The sign shown in the image is the Popeye sign, which occurs due to the rupture of the tendon of the long head of the biceps brachii. On flexing the elbow, the characteristic deformity appears due to the bulging of the muscle belly caused by the contraction of the unrestrained muscles. Hill-Sachs sign refers to a defect in the postero-lateral surface of the humeral head due to repeated impact against the anterior glenoid rim (complication of anterior shoulder dislocation). Griesinger sign refers to oedema of the postauricular soft tissues overlying the mastoid process as a result of thrombosis of the mastoid emissary vein (complication of acute mastoiditis). Rising sun sign (Schwartze sign) is the otoscopic sign of active otospongiosis.

questions.question_text: A patient presented with an enlarged inguinal lymph node. The most likely primary cause is
questions.explanation: The most likely primary cause of an enlarged inguinal lymph node is anal cancer. Lymphatics from the anal canal distal to the dentate line (anal margin) drains into the inguinal lymph nodes. Hence, anal cancer arising distal to the dentate line can cause enlarged inguinal lymph nodes. Lymphatics proximal to the dentate line drains into internal iliac lymph nodes through superior rectal lymphatics. In testicular cancer, lymphatic spread occurs to the paraaortic lymph nodes. In sigmoid colon cancer, lymphatic spread occurs to regional and then central lymph nodes. In prostate cancer, lymphatic spread occurs to internal iliac and external iliac lymph nodes.

questions.question_text: Identify the type of cartilage shown in the image below:
questions.image_description: Histological image showing elastic cartilage with chondrocytes in lacunae surrounded by a matrix rich in dark-staining elastic fibers, with a visible perichondrium at the periphery.
questions.explanation: The image shows elastic cartilage. The elastic cartilage, like hyaline cartilage, contains individual or small groups of chondrocytes surrounded by type II collagen fibrils. However, it differs from hyaline cartilage in that much of the interterritorial matrix consists of fine yellow elastic fibres containing elastin protein. On microscopy, elastic cartilage consists of chondrocytes within lacunae, elastic fibres in the matrix, and perichondrium with outer fibrous layer and inner cellular layer.
